text:
A new set of bis-Schiff base analogues incorporating a thiobarbituric acid moiety were synthesized and investigated towards their applications in hypoglycemic agents. The derivatives were synthesized through a multistep methodology and identified via various spectroscopic tools (HR-ESI-MS, $^{1}\text{H}$ NMR and $^{13}\text{C}$ NMR). They were subsequently evaluated towards *in vitro* $\alpha$-glucosidase inhibition. Except one of the compounds, rest of the analogues exhibited better $\text{IC}_{50}$ value compared with the well-known pharmaceutical agent acarbose ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.27 \pm 0.04\ \mu\text{M}$). Out of all synthesized derivatives compounds **8** ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.10 \pm 0.05\ \mu\text{M}$) and **9** ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.13 \pm 0.03\ \mu\text{MU}$) showed relatively more inhibitory activity that bears chlorine atoms which is negatively charged functional group thereby suggests positive correlation between $0$th order hardness and $\alpha$-glucosidase inhibitory activity.

Structure and activity relationships along with potential molecular targets of synthesized hybrids were investigated via molecular docking studies and observed strong binding affinities along with several critical interactions located at the active sites of enzymes. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations have been applied at B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level to obtain insight about molecular hybrids electronically and stably, by means of Reduced Density Gradient (RDG) and DFT-D3 along with intramolecular hydrogen bonding. In addition, FMO analysis and MEP map were made to express the reactivity behaviors of hybrids along with identification of the nucleophilic and electrophilic attacks sites. In-sights showed that thiobarbituric acid-based bis-Schiff bases may be considered as very promising lead compounds for design of more effective drugs towards type 2 diabetes.
